# How is Regenerative Medicine Transforming the Treatment of Chronic Diseases?

## How is Regenerative Medicine Transforming the Treatment of Chronic Diseases?

## Introduction

In recent years, regenerative medicine has emerged as a promising field that is transforming the way we approach the treatment of chronic diseases. Chronic diseases, such as heart disease, diabetes, arthritis, and Alzheimer’s, affect millions of people worldwide and often come with a lifetime of pain and suffering. Traditional treatments for these conditions have focused on managing symptoms and slowing disease progression. However, regenerative medicine offers a paradigm shift in the way we think about chronic illness management. In this blog post, we will explore how regenerative medicine is revolutionizing the treatment of chronic diseases and improving the quality of life for many patients.

### What is Regenerative Medicine?

Before delving into the impact of regenerative medicine on chronic diseases, it’s essential to understand what regenerative medicine is. Regenerative medicine is a multidisciplinary field that aims to restore or enhance the body’s natural healing mechanisms. It utilizes various approaches, including stem cell therapy, tissue engineering, and gene therapy, to promote the repair, replacement, or regeneration of damaged tissues or organs. This approach differs significantly from traditional medicine, which primarily focuses on symptom management.

**What is Chronic Disease?**

Chronic diseases, also known as non-communicable diseases (NCDs), are long-lasting health conditions that generally develop and persist over an extended period of time, often for years or even a lifetime. Unlike acute diseases, which have a sudden onset and typically resolve relatively quickly, chronic diseases tend to progress slowly and may require ongoing medical management and treatment.

**Common examples of chronic diseases include:**

**Regenerative Medicine and Chronic Conditions**

#### **1. Stem Cell Therapy**

Stem cell therapy is one of the most prominent branches of regenerative medicine. Stem cells have the unique ability to develop into various cell types in the body, making them invaluable for repairing damaged tissues. In the context of chronic diseases, stem cell therapy has shown remarkable potential.

For example, in orthopedics, stem cell therapy has been used to treat conditions like [osteoarthritis](https://www.mayoclinic.org/diseases-conditions/osteoarthritis/symptoms-causes/syc-20351925). Patients with chronic joint pain and limited mobility have found relief through injections of their own stem cells into the affected area. These stem cells can promote cartilage regeneration, reduce inflammation, and improve joint function.

#### **2. Tissue Engineering**

Tissue engineering is another key aspect of regenerative medicine that is transforming chronic disease treatment. It involves creating artificial tissues and organs in the lab for transplantation or repairing damaged tissues in the body. One of the most significant breakthroughs in tissue engineering has been in the field of diabetes treatment.

People with diabetes often struggle with insulin production and regulation. Researchers are working on creating artificial pancreas tissues that can produce insulin and regulate blood sugar levels. This approach could potentially eliminate the need for daily insulin injections and offer a long-term solution to managing diabetes.

#### **3. Gene Therapy**

Gene therapy holds immense promise for the treatment of chronic diseases with a genetic basis. Many chronic conditions, such as cystic fibrosis or certain types of cancer, have genetic components that contribute to their development. Regenerative medicine seeks to correct these genetic abnormalities.

By using techniques like CRISPR-Cas9, researchers can edit or replace faulty genes, potentially curing or significantly alleviating the symptoms of chronic genetic diseases. While gene therapy is still in its early stages, it offers hope for patients who have previously had limited treatment options.

**Enhanced Quality of Life and Disease Management**

Regenerative medicine has the potential to enhance the quality of life for individuals living with chronic diseases. By focusing on regenerating damaged tissues and organs, this approach can alleviate symptoms, slow disease progression, and in some cases, provide a cure. Improved quality of life often translates to reduced healthcare costs and a decreased burden on the healthcare system.

**Chronic Disease Treatment at OrthoMiami**

OrthoMiami is a leading provider of regenerative medicine therapies in the Miami area. We specialize in the treatment of chronic musculoskeletal conditions and offer cutting-edge regenerative treatments that can transform the lives of our patients. Our commitment to delivering high-quality care and staying at the forefront of medical advancements makes us a trusted partner in your journey towards better health.

### Regenerative Medicine Doctor in Miami, FL

Regenerative medicine represents a beacon of hope in the realm of chronic disease treatment. With its potential to repair and regenerate damaged tissues, it offers promising solutions for managing chronic conditions and improving the quality of life for countless individuals. Through ongoing research, innovation, and the collaborative efforts of healthcare professionals and researchers, regenerative medicine is set to redefine how we approach and treat chronic diseases. If you’re interested in exploring regenerative medicine for chronic disease treatment, reach out to OrthoMiami at 786-746-8060 or visit our website to learn more. Embrace the future of healthcare and discover a path to a healthier and happier life through regenerative medicine.

Note: Online forms are only for general inquiries/appointments. Please do not submit any medical questions or your personal health information.

URL: https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4

SUMMARY:
The regenerative medicine research field resulted from the convergence of multiple scientific avenues, such as successful culture of cells in the laboratory [3], identification, characterization and differentiation of stem cells [4–7], and an improved understanding of developmental and molecular biology [8], to conceivably allow control of the intracellular and extracellular environment to promote tissue and organ formation in the laboratory (Fig.1). Media coverage and direct advertising of stem cell therapies as well as the political, ethical and religious controversies surrounding human embryonic stem cells, can contribute not only to increased public awareness but also inflated expectations of regenerative medicine products, and there continues to be a significant gap between the perceived and realistic benefits [[31](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR31 "Bubela T, Li MD, Hafez M, Bieber M, Atkins H.

Transforming healthcare through regenerative medicine
=====================================================

*   [Zita M. Jessop](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#auth-Zita_M_-Jessop-Aff1-Aff2-Aff3)[1](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff1),[2](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff2),[3](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff3), 
*   [Ayesha Al-Sabah](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#auth-Ayesha-Al_Sabah-Aff1-Aff3)[1](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff1),[3](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff3), 
*   [Wendy R. Francis](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#auth-Wendy_R_-Francis-Aff3)[3](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff3)&
*   …
*   [Iain S. Whitaker](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#auth-Iain_S_-Whitaker-Aff1-Aff2-Aff3)[1](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff1),[2](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff2),[3](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Aff3)

Show authors
[_BMC Medicine_](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/)**volume 14**, Article number:115 (2016) [Cite this article](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#citeas)

*   9109 Accesses

*   30 Citations

*   4 Altmetric

*   [Metrics details](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4/metrics)

Abstract
--------

Regenerative medicine therapies, underpinned by the core principles of rejuvenation, regeneration and replacement, are shifting the paradigm in healthcare from symptomatic treatment in the 20th century to curative treatment in the 21st century. By addressing the reasons behind the rapid expansion of regenerative medicine research and presenting an overview of current clinical trials, we explore the potential of regenerative medicine to reshape modern healthcare.

Background
----------

The current dilemmas for modern day healthcare, such as an aging population and the increasing prevalence of chronic diseases, require solutions that limit organ dysfunction and tissue degeneration and which potentially offer replacement. This was first addressed through transplantation, a field that advanced rapidly in the 1950s through a combination of surgical innovations and fundamental scientific breakthroughs in immunosuppression [[1](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR1 "Calne R. The history and development of organ transplantation: biology and rejection. Baillieres Clin Gastroenterol. 1994;8:389–97.")]. In contrast to the allogenic replacement of transplantation, regenerative medicine seeks to apply stem cell research with developmental biology principles to regenerate cells, tissues and organs de novo [[2](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR2 "Haseltine W. Regenerative medicine 2003: an overview. J Regener Med. 2003;4:15–8.")].

The regenerative medicine research field resulted from the convergence of multiple scientific avenues, such as successful culture of cells in the laboratory [[3](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR3 "Landecker H. Culturing Life. Cambridge, MA: Harvard University Press; 2009.")], identification, characterization and differentiation of stem cells [[4](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR4 "Stevens LC. The development of transplantable teratocarcinomas from intratesticular grafts of pre- and postimplantation mouse embryos. Developmen Biol. 1970;21:364–82.")–[7](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR7 "Takahashi K, Yamanaka S. Induction of pluripotent stem cells from mouse embryonic and adult fibroblast cultures by defined factors. Cell. 2006;126:663–76.")], and an improved understanding of developmental and molecular biology [[8](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR8 "Sunderland ME. Studying Development: The Value of Diversity, Theory, and Synthesis. PhD Thesis. Tempe, AZ: Arizona State University; 2008.")], to conceivably allow control of the intracellular and extracellular environment to promote tissue and organ formation in the laboratory (Fig.[1](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Fig1)).

**Fig. 1**

[![Image 5: figure 1](https://media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1186%2Fs12916-016-0669-4/MediaObjects/12916_2016_669_Fig1_HTML.gif)](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4/figures/1)

Regenerative medicine origins

[Full size image](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4/figures/1)

Regenerative medicine has been recognized worldwide as a developing research field that offers the potential to revolutionize patient care in the 21 st century [[9](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR9 "O’Dowd A. Peers call for UK to harness “enormous” potential of regenerative medicine. BMJ. 2013;347:f4248.")]. The prospect of addressing massive healthcare markets, such as cardiovascular disease, neurological conditions or chronic metabolic diseases (e.g. end-stage renal disease or diabetes), means that there has been sustained scientific, public and commercial interest despite early setbacks and slow progress.

Expansion and potential impact of regenerative medicine
-------------------------------------------------------

Demand for regenerative medicine products has been driven by an increase in degenerative and chronic diseases which place cost pressures on healthcare providers, combined with advances in new technologies such as nanotechnology, bioengineering and stem cell therapy [[10](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR10 "Mason C, Dunnill P. The strong financial case for regenerative medicine and the regen industry. Regen Med. 2008;3(3):351–63.")]. Long-term cell, tissue and organ replacement will not only provide an alternative to transplantation [[11](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR11 "Orlando G, Wood KJ, Stratta RJ, Yoo JJ, Atala A, Soker S. Regenerative medicine and organ transplantation: past, present, and future. Transplantation. 2011;91(12):1310–7.")], but will also provide therapeutic options for degenerative conditions (e.g. neurodegenerative conditions (Parkinson’s), stroke and heart failure), which are currently only managed through palliation [[12](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR12 "Tsukamoto A, Abbot SE, Kadyk LC, DeWitt ND, Schaffer DV, Wertheim JA, Whittlesey KJ, Werner MJ. Challenging regeneration to transform medicine. Stem Cells Transl Med. 2006;5(1):1–7."), [13](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR13 "Nelson TJ, Behfar A, Terzic A. Strategies for therapeutic repair: the “R3” regenerative medicine paradigm. Clin Transl Sci. 2008;1:168–71.")].

According to the World Regenerative Medicines Market forecast for 2013–2020 [[14](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR14 "World Regenerative Medicines Market - Opportunities and Forecasts, 2013–2020. Allied Market Research Report. 2014. 
                    https://www.alliedmarketresearch.com/regenerative-medicines-market
                    
                  . Accessed 6 July 2016.")], the global regenerative medicines market for small molecules and biologics, gene therapy and cell therapy is expected to reach $67.5 billion by 2020, which is an increase of $51.1 billion from 2013, thus reflecting its commercial potential. Governments across Europe and the US, as well as their medical research councils, have identified tissue engineering and regenerative medicine at the top of their research priorities [[9](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR9 "O’Dowd A. Peers call for UK to harness “enormous” potential of regenerative medicine. BMJ. 2013;347:f4248.")]. Removal of previous restrictions in embryonic stem cell research in 2009 by the Obama organization is predicted to contribute to further considerable growth within the field as well as improved potential for clinical translation [[15](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR15 "Remarks of President Barack Obama. The White House. 2009. 
                    https://www.whitehouse.gov/the-press-office/remarks-president-prepared-delivery-signing-stem-cell-executive-order-and-scientifi
                    
                  . Accessed 6 July 2016.")].

Clinical trials in regenerative medicine
----------------------------------------

The expansion of regenerative medicine as a scientific discipline, with its core principles of rejuvenation, regeneration and replacement (the 3R’s), is shifting the paradigm in healthcare from symptomatic treatment in the 20th century to curative treatment in the 21st century [[13](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR13 "Nelson TJ, Behfar A, Terzic A. Strategies for therapeutic repair: the “R3” regenerative medicine paradigm. Clin Transl Sci. 2008;1:168–71.")]. This is evidenced by the rapid increase in regenerative medicine clinical trials in each specialty [[16](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR16 "Trounson A. New perspectives in human stem cell therapeutic research. BMC Med. 2009;7:29."), [17](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR17 "Trounson A, Thakar RG, Lomax G, Gibbons D. Clinical trials for stem cell therapies. BMC Med. 2011;9:52.")], which can be broadly classified as using either cell- or tissue-based products (Table[1](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Tab1)). The Food and Drug Administration in the US and the European Medicines Agency have more complex classification systems of regenerative medicine products, including cellular therapy, gene therapy, stimulators of endogenous repair, biologic-device combination products, and human tissue and xenotransplantation [[18](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR18 "Lee MH, Arcidiacono JA, Bilek AM, Wille JJ, Hamill CA, Wonnacott KM, Wells MA, Oh SS. Considerations for tissue-engineered and regenerative medicine product development prior to clinical trials in the United States. Tissue Eng Part B Rev. 2010;16(1):41–54.")]. Broadly, the regulatory requirements can be based on the pillars of sterility, stability and potency, and these need to be addressed prior to successful clinical translation in the future (Table[2](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Tab2)).

**Table 1 Applications of regenerative medicine therapies in different medical specialties**

[Full size table](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4/tables/1)

**Table 2 Overview of testing of regenerative medicine products to validate sterility, stability and potency**

[Full size table](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4/tables/2)

Cell-based therapies work either via stimulation of endogenous repair through extracellular factors or differentiation and functional replacement of endogenous cell types [[17](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR17 "Trounson A, Thakar RG, Lomax G, Gibbons D. Clinical trials for stem cell therapies. BMC Med. 2011;9:52.")]; they include stem cell implantation or infusion to treat hematopoietic diseases, cardiac conditions and Parkinson’s disease. Most of the pioneering work has been performed using haematopoietic stem cells due to the early bone marrow transplant work, making them the most well-studied stem cell type [[19](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR19 "Ford CE, Hamerton JL, Barnes DW, Loutit JF. Cytological identification of radiation-chimaeras. Nature. 1956;177:452–4.")]. In particular, adult mesenchymal stem cells have gained interest as they avoid the ethical concerns of using embryonic stem cells, can be rapidly expanded in vitro and avoid immunogenicity. Studies have shown contradictory results on the efficacy of the transplanted cells, with patient variability with regards to response (Table[1](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Tab1)); further work is needed to elucidate cell identity and health to ensure patient safety (Table[2](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#Tab2)).

The tissue engineering strand of regenerative medicine incorporates cells with biodegradable scaffolds to engineer replacement tissues like dermis or cartilage [[20](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR20 "Cao Y, Vacanti JP, Paige KT, Upton J, Vacanti CA. Transplantation of chondrocytes utilizing a polymer-cell construct to produce tissue-engineered cartilage in the shape of a human ear. Plast Reconstr Surg. 1997;100:297–304.")] and whole organs such as trachea and bladder [[21](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR21 "Macchiarini P, Jungebluth P, Go T, Asnaghi MA, Rees LE, Cogan TA, Dodson A, Martorell J, Bellini S, Parnigotto PP, Dickinson SC, Hollander AP, Mantero S, Conconi MT, Birchall MA. Clinical transplantation of a tissue-engineered airway. Lancet. 2008;372:2023–30."), [22](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR22 "Atala A, Bauer SB, Soker S, Yoo JJ, Retic AB. Tissue-engineered autologous bladders for patients needing cystoplasty. Lancet. 2006;367:1241–6.")]. Limitations of synthetic polymer scaffolds, such as infection, extrusion and degradation product toxicity, have encouraged interest in decellularised matrices as well biologics for use as scaffolds as one of the more effective ways of replicating native tissue anisotropy [[21](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR21 "Macchiarini P, Jungebluth P, Go T, Asnaghi MA, Rees LE, Cogan TA, Dodson A, Martorell J, Bellini S, Parnigotto PP, Dickinson SC, Hollander AP, Mantero S, Conconi MT, Birchall MA. Clinical transplantation of a tissue-engineered airway. Lancet. 2008;372:2023–30."), [22](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR22 "Atala A, Bauer SB, Soker S, Yoo JJ, Retic AB. Tissue-engineered autologous bladders for patients needing cystoplasty. Lancet. 2006;367:1241–6.")]. Decellularised matrices provide durability, enhanced integration and biocompatibility whilst avoiding allosensitization [[21](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR21 "Macchiarini P, Jungebluth P, Go T, Asnaghi MA, Rees LE, Cogan TA, Dodson A, Martorell J, Bellini S, Parnigotto PP, Dickinson SC, Hollander AP, Mantero S, Conconi MT, Birchall MA. Clinical transplantation of a tissue-engineered airway. Lancet. 2008;372:2023–30.")]. This may explain why many of the significant breakthroughs and first-in-man studies have utilized this technique combined with autologous cell-seeding with some success [[21](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR21 "Macchiarini P, Jungebluth P, Go T, Asnaghi MA, Rees LE, Cogan TA, Dodson A, Martorell J, Bellini S, Parnigotto PP, Dickinson SC, Hollander AP, Mantero S, Conconi MT, Birchall MA. Clinical transplantation of a tissue-engineered airway. Lancet. 2008;372:2023–30.")–[23](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR23 "Gonfiotti A, Jaus MO, Barale D, Baiguera S, Comin C, Lavorini F, Fontana G, Sibila O, Rombolà G, Jungebluth P, Macchiarini P. The first tissue-engineered airway transplantation: 5-year follow-up results. Lancet. 2014;383(9913):238–44.")], and even showed promise in vitro for more complex structures such as pulmonary and aortic valves as well as whole organs such as heart and liver [[24](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR24 "Ott HC, Matthiesen TS, Goh SK, Black LD, Kren SM, Netoff TI. Perfusion-decellularized matrix: using nature’s platform to engineer a bioartificial heart. Nat Med. 2008;14(2):213–21."), [25](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR25 "Soto-Gutierrez A, Zhang L, Medberry C, Fukumitsu K, Faulk D, Jiang H. A whole-organ regenerative medicine approach for liver replacement. Tissue Eng Part C Methods. 2011;17(6):677–86.")]. However, despite early interest and investment in tissue engineering research, with annual R&D spending estimated at US$580 million, initial clinically applicable product release has been slow but steady [[26](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR26 "Kemp P. History of regenerative medicine: looking backwards to move forwards. Regen Med. 2006;1(5):653–69.")].

Controversies in the field
--------------------------

The regenerative medicine field has been shrouded in controversy. Significant potential gains have led to several high profile allegations of research misconduct [[27](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR27 "Cyranoski D. Acid bath offers easy path to stem cells. Nature News. 2014. 
                    http://www.nature.com/news/acid-bath-offers-easy-path-to-stem-cells-1.14600
                    
                  . Accessed 6 July 2016"), [28](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR28 "Vogel G. Regenerative medicine. Report finds misconduct by surgeon. Science. 2015;348(6238):954–5.")]. There is also a growing stem cell tourism industry based on unproven treatments that aims to capitalize on stem cell hype [[29](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR29 "Alta CR. On the road (to a cure?) — Stem-cell tourism and lessons for gene editing. N Engl J Med. 2016;374:901–3."), [30](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR30 "Matthews KR, Iltis AS. Unproven stem cell-based interventions and achieving a compromise policy among the multiple stakeholders. BMC Med Ethics. 2015;16:75.")]. Desperate patients would rather approach private clinics offering experimental stem cell treatments, with unproven safety and efficacy profiles, than wait for outcomes of clinical trials [[30](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR30 "Matthews KR, Iltis AS. Unproven stem cell-based interventions and achieving a compromise policy among the multiple stakeholders. BMC Med Ethics. 2015;16:75.")]. Media coverage and direct advertising of stem cell therapies as well as the political, ethical and religious controversies surrounding human embryonic stem cells, can contribute not only to increased public awareness but also inflated expectations of regenerative medicine products, and there continues to be a significant gap between the perceived and realistic benefits [[31](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR31 "Bubela T, Li MD, Hafez M, Bieber M, Atkins H. Is belief larger than fact: expectations, optimism and reality for translational stem cell research. BMC Med. 2012;10:133.")]. A concerted effort from the scientific community as well as robust outcome data from clinical trials will be needed to temper unrealistic claims [[16](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR16 "Trounson A. New perspectives in human stem cell therapeutic research. BMC Med. 2009;7:29."), [17](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR17 "Trounson A, Thakar RG, Lomax G, Gibbons D. Clinical trials for stem cell therapies. BMC Med. 2011;9:52.")].

Conclusion
----------

Medical breakthroughs often require the convergence of multiple scientific advances for which interdisciplinary collaboration is fundamental. Similar to transplant medicine, regenerative medicine requires the convergence of a number of scientific disciplines, including stem cell biology, developmental and molecular biology, engineering and biomaterials. Despite media hype, scientific overclaim and unrealistic expectations, which have been previously witnessed for a number of healthcare technologies, regenerative medicine continues to make steady progress reflected by the increasing number of clinical trials [[16](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR16 "Trounson A. New perspectives in human stem cell therapeutic research. BMC Med. 2009;7:29."), [17](https://bmcmedicine.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/s12916-016-0669-4#ref-CR17 "Trounson A, Thakar RG, Lomax G, Gibbons D. Clinical trials for stem cell therapies. BMC Med. 2011;9:52.")]. Significant potential has been demonstrated in the cell therapy field to treat haematological, neurological and rheumatological conditions. The tissue engineering field, although holding great promise, still has some way to develop before the excitement surrounding novel biofabrication strategies, such as 3D bioprinting, is translated to patient care. The fast moving and versatile field of regenerative medicine is at the cutting edge of translational research and could shift the paradigm in healthcare from symptomatic to curative treatment. _BMC Medicine_ is very interested in breakthroughs in regenerative medicine/stem cell therapy and submission of such relevant articles is encouraged.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Regenerative medicine introduces innovative therapies that target root causes of diseases, potentially transforming chronic disease management from symptom relief to curative interventions.
By utilizing advanced techniques like stem cell therapy and tissue engineering, regenerative medicine offers promising solutions for conditions like diabetes, arthritis, and heart disease.
Preventing disease progression through regenerative therapies can significantly decrease long-term healthcare expenses associated with chronic disease management and hospitalizations.
Regenerative medicine enhances healing processes, leading to quicker recovery times and improved outcomes for patients suffering from acute and chronic conditions.
By restoring function and alleviating symptoms, regenerative medicine not only extends life but also improves the overall quality of life for patients.
